questions.question: Explain the difference between anonymous pipes and named pipes (FIFOs).
keyPoints: Anonymous pipes exist only during process lifetime, FIFOs persist in filesystem
Anonymous pipes require parent-child relationship, FIFOs allow unrelated processes
FIFOs have pathname visible in filesystem, anonymous pipes don't
FIFOs can be accessed by name, anonymous pipes use file descriptors

questions.question: What is PIPE_BUF and why is it important for FIFO operations?
keyPoints: PIPE_BUF is 4096 bytes in Linux
Defines maximum atomic write size
Writes smaller than PIPE_BUF are guaranteed atomic
Writes larger than PIPE_BUF may interleave between multiple writers

questions.question: Describe the blocking behavior when opening and FIFOs.
keyPoints: Opening FIFO for reading blocks until writer opens
Opening FIFO for writing blocks until reader opens
read blocks if no data available
write blocks if no reader (buffer full)

questions.question: How can deadlock occur when using FIFOs and how can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Deadlock occurs when both processes wait for each other
Both ends may block waiting to open
Prevention: Open in correct order (reader then writer)
Use O_NONBLOCK flag
Create two FIFOs for bidirectional communication

questions.question: Write the procedure for client-server communication using FIFOs.
keyPoints: Server creates request FIFO (well-known)
Server creates unique response FIFO for each client
Clients open request FIFO to send requests
Clients create personal response FIFO for replies
Server reads from request FIFO, responds via client's response FIFO

questions.question: What are the different ways to create and remove FIFOs?
keyPoints: Shell: mkfifo command
Programmatically: mkfifo system call
Removal: rm command
Removal: unlink system call
Permissions set with -m option in mkfifo

questions.question: Write a C program to create a FIFO, demonstrate producer-consumer problem using FIFOs.
keyPoints: Use mkfifo to create FIFO
Open FIFO in appropriate mode
Producer writes data to FIFO
Consumer reads data from FIFO
Handle blocking and error conditions
Proper cleanup with close and unlink

questions.question: Explain the various flags and modes used with FIFOs. Discuss O_NONBLOCK and O_RDONLY/O_WRONLY modes.
keyPoints: O_RDONLY: Open for reading only
O_WRONLY: Open for writing only
O_RDWR: Open for both (rarely used for FIFOs)
O_NONBLOCK: Non-blocking mode
O_CREAT: Create if doesn't exist (for mkfifo)
How blocking behavior differs with flags

questions.question: Design a chat system between two processes using FIFOs. Explain the setup and show how bidirectional communication is achieved.
keyPoints: Need two FIFOs for bidirectional communication
Process A writes to FIFO1, reads from FIFO2
Process B writes to FIFO2, reads from FIFO1
Must handle deadlock by opening FIFOs in correct order
Use separate threads or non-blocking for simultaneous communication

questions.question: Compare FIFOs with other IPC mechanisms like message queues, sockets, and shared memory.
keyPoints: FIFOs: Simple, file-based, unidirectional
Message Queues: Similar but with message boundaries, priorities
Sockets: Network capable, bidirectional, more overhead
Shared Memory: Fastest but requires synchronization
FIFOs best for simple producer-consumer scenarios
